SnowDistribution: Steep, good snow ice conditions, mostly icy at higher elevations. No visibility throughout the day above 600 metres. Winds 30 -40 mph, progress difficult and tiring, easing later.
Comment: Becoming cooler, old snow will be very icy.
WeatherInfluences: Freezing level will be around the glens overnight before lifting back up to 900 metres during the day. Snowfall during the night otherwise clear. A strong breeze from the North-North-West, veering North and easing.
SnowStability: New localised accumulations will develop in wind sheltered terrain mainly on North-East to South-East aspects. Above 900 metres where accumulations achieve depth over older snow-ice they will be moderately bonded. Gully tops and corrie rims will be most affected. Elsewhere, old snow will be firm and often icy with greatest amounts above 900 metres.  The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: A mild, dry day with the cloud base initially around 650 metres.  Winds were very strong from the South, easing later in the afternoon.
ObservedSnowStability: The snow cover remains well bonded and stable throughout. Greatest cover is to be found above 900 metres on the high plateau areas and on South-West through West to North aspects. Very patchy cover below this altitude. The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Firm snow. Poor visibility. Easing winds, around 20 mph at midday.
Comment:
The firm snowpack will present a serious hazard in the event of a trip or slip.
Greatest snow cover currently on North-West through North to East aspects above 750 metres.
WeatherInfluences: Winds become light overnight before increasing from the South-South-West during the day. It is expected to remain dry with an overnight frost followed by a cool day.
SnowStability: The older snowpack will refreeze and have good stability. Localised and shallow areas of unstable windslab will persist mainly on North-East to South-East aspects above 800 metres with features such as Gully tops and corrie rims most affected. There will be some redistribution of this windslab onto North and North-East aspects during the day. The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Strong Westerly winds. Rain gradually turned to snow through the day before becoming mainly dry later in the afternoon. The freezing level dropped to around 700 metres.
ObservedSnowStability: The existing snowpack was starting to refreeze and has good stability. Localised and shallow areas of fresh, unstable windslab were developing mainly on North-East to South-East aspects above 800 metres, with features such as gully tops an corrie rims most affected. The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Diminishing snowpack, better above 850 metres.  Cloud base was at 750 metres all day.  Winds 20 -30 mph progress difficult at times with stronger gusts
WeatherInfluences: A little light snowfall very early in the period then cold and dry with light variable winds.
SnowStability: Good stability in all locations. The diminished snowpack will refreeze and become hard or crusty at all elevations. Pockets of thin windslab may be in place on some steep East to South East aspects above 950m.  The avalanche hazard will be Low
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Mild and wet but with some late snow showers near summits. Moderate South West winds.
ObservedSnowStability: The diminished snowpack was moist or wet at all altitudes at the time of reporting. Stability is good in most locations. Residual cornices are wet and weak.  The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Thaw conditions in the morning, some fresh snow on the higher summits from midday.  Cloud base around 700 metres most of the day. Winds 20 -30 mph.
Comment: As the existing snowpack refreezes the snow surface will become very firm with the potential for serious consequences in the event of a slip or trip.
WeatherInfluences: A cold, dry sunny day. The freezing level will be around 700 metres. Moderate Northerly winds will ease overnight becoming light Southerly by dawn and increasing in strength slightly through the day.
SnowStability: The older snowpack will remain well bonded and stable in all locations. Isolated deposits of shallow windslab will persist at the top of some steep North-East to South-East aspects above 1100 metres. These deposits will be confined to wind sheltered areas such and gully tops and coire rims and gradually consolidate through the day. The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Rain at all levels overnight and through the morning  gradually turned to snow above around 750 metres and eased through the afternoon. Strong Southerly winds moderated and became North-Westerly.
ObservedSnowStability: The existing snowpack remains well bonded and stable. Isolated deposits of moderately bonded windslab are developing on some steep North-East to South-East aspects above around 1100 metres. These deposits are shallow and generally confined to some wind sheltered areas such as some gully tops and coire rims. The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Very firm snow in most locations. Cloud base was around 750 metres for most of the day with poor visibility above this elevation. Moderate, fresh at times Westerly winds were around 15 -20 mph. with rain showers in the morning some light snow fall in the afternoon .
Comment: Largely dry and settled through Friday evening and Saturday, with just a little rain possible. Freezing levels rising again late Saturday.
WeatherInfluences: Early evening Thursday, the freezing level lowering to around 700 metres with some light new snow on fresh Westerly winds. These winds will ease becoming Southerly and then South-Westerly and dry conditions are expected. with a slightly rising freezing levels.
SnowStability: New snow accumulations are not expected to be significant. The older moist snowpack will start to re-freeze and consolidate it will be well bonded, very firm and stable. Largest continuous snow areas remain on North-West through North to East aspects above around 870 metres. The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: The freezing level has been above the summits for nearly 24 hours it has gradually lowered this morning to be zero degrees at 1100 metres around midday. Rain at all levels through this morning accompanied by moderate to fresh Westerly winds.
ObservedSnowStability: A light dusting of new snow arrived in the afternoon these accumulations are not extensive and lie mainly above 950 metres.. The older moist snowpack is well bonded, very firm and stable. Largest continuous snow areas remain on North-West through North to East aspects above around 870 metres. The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Winds approx 40 - 60 mph easing through the afternoon.  Cloudy on the tops with some bright spells in the morning, lowering through the afternoon.  Firm snow with a thawing surface layer on most aspects at higher levels.
Comment: Rock fall will continue to be a potential hazard.
WeatherInfluences: Snowfall is expected above 400 to 500 metres on fresh North-West to North-North-East winds, this will clear to leave a dry day with light increasing to fresh Southerly winds. The 500 metre freezing level will steadily rise to summit levels by the end of the day.
SnowStability: Localised accumulations of windslab will develop above 900 metres on East to South aspects these will be isolated on South-West aspects. The greatest instability will be where accumulations gain depth in steep terrain such as gully tops, coire rims and convexities. Later in the period these accumulations will begin to consolidate. On these aspects fresh fragile cornices will develop. Elsewhere the old snowpack will be firm well bonded and stable. The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Mostly dry through the morning before the onset of rain and drizzle through the afternoon, this turned to snowfall above 1000 metres later in the day. Winds were gale force Southerly, The freezing level at midday was around summit levels, cooler through the afternoon.
ObservedSnowStability: The snowpack surface is thawing and is generally stable on all aspects and elevations. The greatest snow cover remains in high coires and plateau areas above 800 metres, with snow patches and ribbons extending to lower elevations. Glide cracks were observed on a steep North facing slope at around 1000 metres. Rockfall continues to be a potential hazard. The avalanche hazard is Low.
